FNP 03/20/2026 Nom eClinicalWarks Sollware Generated for Printing/Faxing/eTransmitting on: 03/23/2026 12:01 PM PDT 4/8 3/23/26, 12:01 PM Print Preview TUIA, Alfred S DOB: 12/02/1978 (47 yo M) Acc No. 35069 DOS: 03/20/2026 47-year-old male with chronic bilateral knee pain. Pt is stable on current medication regimen with occasional knee injections. Suspect left shoulder pain is due to labral pathology, rotator cuff pathology, and adhesive capsulitis confirmed on MR L Shoulder. He has very limited exam. Unable to tolerate conservative measures. Will send to Ortho for further management. Has current mental illness and is currently on disability followed by psychiatrists Chronic bilateral knee pain, reportedly unable to get knee replacement due to insurance S/Pleft knee injection on 10/7/22 with 50% relief S/P 3/6/24 bilateral knee genicular RFA. Patient reports pain relief of 40 % on the left knee and still with moderate pain on the right knee. Explained patient that best result is between 4-6 weeks after RFA-s/p bilateral knee"viscosupplementation injections" for bilateral knee DJD. Recommend doing these in a series of 3; 1 week apart -Patient is status post 7/12/2023, 7/18/23, 8/1/23 Pt reports good relief with the injection/ his knee's don't swell as much. Left knee is doing better than the right -s/p right genicular block on 9/6/22 with great relief nearing her more and more ease with walking and range of motion s/p bilateral Knee RFA genicular nerve block 30% pain relief on L knee. R knee medial part has improved but lateral part has not. S/P Bilateral Lumbar MBB L3, L4, L5 10/16/24 - reports 80% pain relief for 2 weeks. -S/P Bilateral Lumbar MBB L345 12/3/24 with 80% relief for at least the duration of the anesthetic S/P 1/8/25 bilateral L3,L4,L5 RFA with 60% reduction pain for 6 months S/P 07/15/25 bilateral L3,L4,L5 RFA with 50% reduction pain Plan: -Suspect non radiating low back pain is due to lumbar spondylosis confirmed on MR L Spine -ORDERed referral to Dr. Cameron King with OIH for his left shoulder -Ordered bilateral L5-S1 lumbar facet fusion. The patient has chronic radicular low back pain which has remained refractory to 6-12 months of conservative measures including medications, therapies, and spine interventions. Patient MRI reveals retrolisthesis L5 on S1. Disc desiccated with disc high narrowing and facet arthropathy. Hx of coronary artery disease. Patient on Brilinta and Aspirin. Patient needs cardiologist clearance prior facet fusion. Clearance form printed and given to the patient -Order flexion/extension x-rays lumbar. Rule out lumbar instability -Pending of Cardiologist clearance, EKG, lab work Medications -Continue Hydrocodone/Acetaminophen 10/325 mg q 4-6 hours PRN for severe pain # 150 ( for opioid rotation with Oxycodone) -Hold Oxycodone 10mg q 4-6 hours PRN for severe pain #130 Progress Note: Carlos Nunez Cendan. The urine drug testing for this patient is based on individual risk potential which I believe is in a low risk category for this patient with anticipated twice yearly screens. Treatment 1. Bilateral primary osteoarthritis of knee Notes: I discussed the risks of opioid medications for pain management. These risks include addiction, substance abuse, medication tolerance, physical dependence, respiratory depression, constipation, nausea, and an opioid-induced hyperalgesic state (increase in pain from opioid medications). The patient has been warned to keep their medication in a safe place. Proper use, storage, and disposal of the medication was discussed. They have also been told not to drink alcohol while using the medication. The patient has been informed that The Center for Wellness and Pain Care of Las Vegas conforms to all of the recommendations of the Nevada Medical Board. Specific risks were discussed with the patient including not operating a vehicle while taking opioids because of increased risk of a motor vehicle accident. We discussed how Progress Note: Carlos Nunez Cendan. The patient denies any abuse of the medications. The patient does not have any signs of opioid withdrawal or excessive sleepiness. The patient believes the medication is helping to control the pain. The patient has signed an opioid contract and states they are only taking the medication as prescribed. Discussed the common and serious side effects of this medication and the need to alert me if experiencing these or having any concerns about a possible medication effect. Common side-effects may include drowsiness, nausea, weight gain, imbalance, thinking abnormalities, seizures, depression/suicide and other side-effects. Advised the patient to also discuss possible side effects, contraindications, and interactions with their pharmacist. Patient presents for a refill of current pain medications. Patient states medications provide effective pain control, and improve quality of life by at least 30%. Patient notes no serious side effects, and feels the benefits of therapy outweigh the risks. Given the patient's stability on the current medication regimen, we did refill the medications today. Patient's Nevada State Board of Pharmacy Controlled Substances Prescription Monitoring Program report was reviewed today. Patient will perform a urine drug screen today, with most recent prior UDS performed within the last three months. An opioid risk tool was also utilized to determine opioid overdose risk profile. Also, the appropriate use of Narcan was discussed and prescription was offered on today's visit if not already offered previously. 4.
